mappings.rationaleAI: The samurai class from its rise through the Genpei Wars and Kamakura shogunate, bushido code, the Tokugawa transformation from warriors to administrators, and abolition during the Meiji Restoration correspond to C.1 (Kamakura period), C.4 (Tokugawa period), and C.4.d (weakening of the bakuhan system).

Japan's Sakoku period (c. 1633-1853), the Tokugawa edicts restricting foreign contact, the Dutch trading post at Dejima, and the eventual opening by Commodore Perry directly correspond to C.4.a (national seclusion policy) and C.4.d.iii (Tempo reforms and European/US pressure).

The Shimabara Rebellion (1637-1638), the Christian-led uprising against the Tokugawa shogunate, and its consequences including the eradication of Christianity and establishment of sakoku directly correspond to C.3.b (arrival of Portuguese and Catholic missionaries) and C.4.a (national seclusion policy).

The Korean Empire (1897-1910), the Sino-Japanese and Russo-Japanese Wars' impact on Korean sovereignty, and Japan's annexation in 1910 directly correspond to D.5 (Yi dynasty) and D.5.b (Japanese invasions and Korea as a Ch'ing vassal).

Zen Buddhism's influence on Japanese culture (tea ceremony, calligraphy, garden design, martial arts), the Rinzai and Soto schools, correspond to C.1.d (Buddhist culture during the Kamakura period, growth of Zen) and C.3.c (influence of Zen Buddhism on philosophy, drama, and the arts).

Confucian influence across East Asia, including Korea and Japan, and Neo-Confucianism are relevant to D.5.a (establishment of a Confucian state under the Yi dynasty) and C.4.d.ii (Confucianism and the Shinto revival in Tokugawa Japan).

The Russo-Japanese War (1904-1905) and its impact on Korean sovereignty, with Japan's rise as a great power leading directly to Korea's annexation, corresponds to D.5.b on Japanese and Manchu invasions and their impact on Korea.

Romance of the Three Kingdoms' enormous cultural influence in Japan and Korea provides context for understanding East Asian literary culture, relevant to D.2 (cultural developments in Three Kingdoms-era Korean history).
